title: practical english usage - Part 51
content:
The company has been losing money recently.

last three days.

time continuing up to now, like recently, lately, this week, since January, for the We   often use the present perfect progressive with words that refer to a period of 5 time    expressions:        recently,         lately,      this    week,      since      ..., for   . .., etc I've been waking up in the night a lot. I think I'll see the doctor.

People have been phoning me all day.

also for repeated activity.

We   can use the present perfect progressive not only for continuous activity, but 4 repeated       actions I must just go and wash. I've been gardening.

Sorry I'm late. Have you been waiting long?

'You look hot.' 'Yes, I've been running.' have just stopped, but which  have present results.

We   also use the present perfect progressive for actions and situations which 3 actions      and   situations       that    have    just   stopped Section5 Past   and   Perfect     Tenses grammar •  51  present perfect simple or progressive?

Look at all the rose bushes I’ ve planted! (     .. . I’ve been planting. ) - I’ ve been planting rose bushes all afternoon.

we  have done something. Compare: We   use the simple present perfect to say how  much we  have done, or how  often 3 how     much?      how     often?      simple     present      perfect beginning of time.

Scientists believe that the universe has been expanding steadily since the development, even if this is permanent.

We   generally use the progressive to talk about continuous change or Harry has been working / has worked in the same Job for thirty years.

It’s been raining / It’s rained steadily since last Saturday.

difference of emphasis.

Progressive and simple tenses are sometimes both possible, with a slight My parents have lived in Bristol all their lives.

- I’ ve been living at Emma ’s place for the last month.

He hasn’ t worked for years.

- I haven’ t been working very well recently.

My grandmother has played the piano since she was a little girl.

- I’ ve been playing the piano all afternoon, and I’m  really tired.

simple present perfect. Compare: When  we  talk about longer-lasting or permanent situations we  often prefer the We   use progressive forms mostly for shorter, temporary actions and situations.

2 temporary        or  permanent I’ ve only known her for two days. (\’ or I’ve only been knowing her. . .) not She’s had a cold since Monday, (     She^s-beervhetving-a-eold-...) niii Jack’s been ill all week, (      been being ill...) and know.

for which  a progressive form is more suitable. - I’ m  working very hard at the moment.

It’s been raining all day.

- It’s raining.

still going on - for example when we  say how  long it has lasted. Compare: present perfect progressive when we  are looking back over something that is that is going on now started in the past!).
